PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 36110/97) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by four Turkish nationals, Mr Galip Yalman, Mr Bahattin Sarısoy, Mr Osman Çağlayan and Mr Yusuf Çamca (“the applicants”), on 29 November 1996.

The applicants were represented by Mr S. Esmer, a lawyer practising in Ankara. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the Convention institutions.

The applicants alleged that their case, which commenced in 1989 and terminated in 1996, was not heard within a reasonable time as required by the Convention.

The application was transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to the Second Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On 1 November 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed First Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision of 15 May 2003, the Court declared the application admissible.

The applicants and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

In September 1980 the applicants were taken into police custody and were subsequently placed in detention on remand on suspicion of membership of an illegal organisation. The Erzincan Military Public Prosecutor filed a bill of indictment with the Erzincan Martial Law Court (sıkıyönetim askeri mahkemesi) charging the applicants under Article 146 of the Criminal Code with membership of an illegal organisation whose object was to undermine the constitutional order.

On various dates between 1982 and 1984 the applicants were released pending trial.

On 13 September 1988 the Erzincan Martial Law Court acquitted the applicants of the charges against them.

On various dates in March, April and June 1989 the applicants brought individual actions before the Sinop Assize Court against the Treasury, pursuant to Law no. 466. They requested compensation for their unjustified detention on remand.

On 20 October 1989 the court decided to join the applicants’ cases.

On 15 December 1993 the Sinop Assize Court awarded non‑pecuniary compensation to the applicants. The applicants appealed, arguing that the amount of compensation awarded was not sufficient.

On 31 January 1995 the Court of Cassation quashed the judgment of the first instance court.

On 6 June 1995 the Sinop Assize Court increased the amount of non‑pecuniary compensation.

On 15 June 1995 the applicants again appealed.

On 30 May 1996 the Court of Cassation dismissed the applicants’ appeal and upheld the judgment of the assize court.
anonymized_llm:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (bearing a specific reference number) against the respondent State lodged with a relevant international human rights body (“that body”) under former Article 25 of a foundational human rights treaty (“the treaty”) by four nationals of the respondent State, identified as “the applicants”, on a specific date in late November.

The applicants were represented by their legal professional, based in a major city in the respondent State. The authorities of the respondent State did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the institutions established under the treaty.

The applicants alleged that their case, which commenced in the late 1980s and terminated in the mid-1990s, was not heard within a reasonable time as required by the treaty.

The application was transmitted to the central adjudicating body on a specific date in early November, when Protocol No. 11 to the treaty came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to a specific section of the adjudicating body (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of the central adjudicating body). Within that section, the subdivision that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the treaty) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On a specific date in early November several years later, the central adjudicating body changed the composition of its sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to a newly composed section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision of mid-May of a later year, the central adjudicating body declared the application admissible.

The applicants and the respondent State each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

In the autumn of a particular year, the applicants were taken into police custody and were subsequently placed in detention on remand on suspicion of membership of an illegal organisation. A military prosecutor's office filed a bill of indictment with a military court responsible for martial law cases, charging the applicants under Article 146 of the Criminal Code with membership of an illegal organisation whose object was to undermine the constitutional order.

On various dates between the early to mid-1980s, the applicants were released pending trial.

On a specific date in mid-September of a subsequent year, the military court responsible for martial law cases acquitted the applicants of the charges against them.

On various dates in the spring and early summer of that year, the applicants brought individual actions before a court of general jurisdiction against the national financial authority, pursuant to Law no. 466. They requested compensation for their unjustified detention on remand.

In late October of the same year, the court decided to join the applicants’ cases.

In mid-December several years later, the court of general jurisdiction awarded non‑pecuniary compensation to the applicants. The applicants appealed, arguing that the amount of compensation awarded was not sufficient.

In late January of a later year, the highest appellate court quashed the judgment of the first instance court.

In early June of the same year, the court of general jurisdiction increased the amount of non‑pecuniary compensation.

In mid-June of the same year, the applicants again appealed.

In late May of the following year, the highest appellate court dismissed the applicants’ appeal and upheld the judgment of the court of general jurisdiction.

original:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (nos. 43185/98 and 43186/98) against the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by two United Kingdom nationals, John Harding Price (introduced on 4 May 1998) and Mary Hazel Lowe (introduced on 12 August 1998).

The United Kingdom Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr C.A. Whomersley of the Foreign and Commonwealth Office.

The applicants alleged that the proceedings in which they were sued in respect of property in Florida were not dealt with within a reasonable time.

The applications were transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The applications were allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On 1 November 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Second Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision of 3 September 2002 the Court declared the applications partly admissible, having in the meantime joined them on 28 November 2000.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in 1929 and 1923 respectively. The applicant in application no. 43185/98 (“Dr Price”) is a consultant psychiatrist; the applicant in application no. 43186/98 (“Mrs Lowe”) is a medical secretary. They live in Lincoln.

The applicants, together with Dr Price’s wife, were sued by “K”, a former patient of Dr Price, in respect of property in Florida. The writ was issued on 12 February 1986. Before the action came to trial, the plaintiff was adjudicated bankrupt on 9 August 1989 and the trustee in bankruptcy continued the action.

The trial was set down to commence in the High Court in August 1994 but was postponed until the end of September 1994 because the judge was not available for the length of time needed for the hearing.

On the fourth day of trial, while K was being cross-examined, an adjournment was granted because K claimed to feel unwell. In the event, it emerged that no medical justification could be given for his continued non-attendance, but nonetheless he remained absent for the rest of the trial. Because of the delay caused by K’s non-attendance, the trial could not be concluded in September 1994 and had to be adjourned until January 1995. Judgment was given on 27 April 1995.

The judge accepted the argument of the applicants and Mrs Price that it was necessary to disregard K’s evidence because of his failure to remain for cross-examination. However, on the basis of the documentary evidence and that adduced by the applicants and Mrs Price, the judge found that the transfer of the Florida property should be set aside on the ground that the defendants had not been able to rebut the presumption of undue influence created by the discrepancy in the price they paid K for the property and its value at the time of the transfer, together with the doctor-patient relationship between Dr Price and K.

The formal record of the judge’s order was not prepared by the parties until 26 October 1995. The applicants and Mrs Price applied to the Court of Appeal for leave to appeal on 1 November 1995. The appeal bundles were lodged on 4 October 1996. On 15 January 1997 the appeal was listed for hearing, and it was heard on 16 and 17 April 1997, on which last date the Court of Appeal gave judgment dismissing the appeal.

In connection with the moneys paid by Dr Price for the property, the Court of Appeal confirmed the decision of the trial judge that the sum should be repaid, but that no interest should be paid on it. The trial judge had based his decision on the fact that Dr Price “had had possession of the properties and receipt of the rents since the completion of the sale, subject only to the agreement to allow [K] to use number 212 in the early months”. Lord Justice Nourse regarded this as a “rough and ready result”, but one which would “reduce the opportunities for further dispute between the parties”.

On 19 April 1997 the applicants and Mrs Price were refused leave by the Court of Appeal to appeal to the House of Lords. On 30 March 1998 the applicants and Mrs Price were refused leave to appeal by the House of Lords.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (nos. Application A and Application B) against the respondent State, lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by two nationals of the respondent State, Applicant A (introduced in May of that year) and Applicant B (introduced in August of that year).

The Government of the respondent State (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent from the Foreign and Commonwealth Office.

The applicants alleged that the proceedings in which they were sued in respect of property located abroad were not dealt with within a reasonable time.

The applications were transmitted to the Court in early November of that year, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The applications were allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

Three years later, in early November, the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Second Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision in early September of the following year, the Court declared the applications partly admissible, having in the meantime joined them in late November of the prior year.

The applicants and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in the late 1920s and early 1920s respectively. Applicant A is a consultant psychiatrist; Applicant B is a medical secretary. They live in a locality in the region.

The applicants, together with Applicant A's wife, were sued by the claimant, a former patient of Applicant A, in respect of property located abroad. The writ was issued in February of a particular year. Before the action came to trial, the claimant was adjudicated bankrupt in August of a later year and the trustee in bankruptcy continued the action.

The trial was set down to commence in a national court of first instance in August of a subsequent year but was postponed until the end of September of that year because the judge was not available for the length of time needed for the hearing.

On the fourth day of trial, while the claimant was being cross-examined, an adjournment was granted because the claimant claimed to feel unwell. In the event, it emerged that no medical justification could be given for the claimant's continued non-attendance, but nonetheless the claimant remained absent for the rest of the trial. Because of the delay caused by the claimant's non-attendance, the trial could not be concluded in September of that year and had to be adjourned until January of the following year. Judgment was given in late April of that year.

The judge accepted the argument of the applicants and Applicant A's wife that it was necessary to disregard the claimant's evidence because of the claimant's failure to remain for cross-examination. However, on the basis of the documentary evidence and that adduced by the applicants and Applicant A's wife, the judge found that the transfer of the property located abroad should be set aside on the ground that the defendants had not been able to rebut the presumption of undue influence created by the discrepancy in the price they paid the claimant for the property and its value at the time of the transfer, together with the doctor-patient relationship between Applicant A and the claimant.

The formal record of the judge's order was not prepared by the parties until late October of that year. The applicants and Applicant A's wife applied to a national appellate court for leave to appeal in early November of that same year. The appeal bundles were lodged in early October of the following year. In mid-January of the subsequent year the appeal was listed for hearing, and it was heard in mid-April of that same year, on which last date the national appellate court gave judgment dismissing the appeal.

In connection with the moneys paid by Applicant A for the property, the national appellate court confirmed the decision of the trial judge that the sum should be repaid, but that no interest should be paid on it. The trial judge had based the decision on the fact that Applicant A “had had possession of the properties and receipt of the rents since the completion of the sale, subject only to the agreement to allow the claimant to use a specific unit of the property in the early months”. A presiding judge regarded this as a “rough and ready result”, but one which would “reduce the opportunities for further dispute between the parties”.

A few days later, in mid-April, the applicants and Applicant A's wife were refused leave by the national appellate court to appeal to the highest national appellate court. In late March of the following year the applicants and Applicant A's wife were refused leave to appeal by the highest national appellate court.

original:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 42552/98) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by Turkish nationals, Mr Mehmet Bülent Yılmaz and Mr Şahin Yılmaz (“the applicants”), on 20 May 1998.

The applicants, who had been granted legal aid, were represented by Mr H.Ç. Akbulut, a lawyer practising in Izmir. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purpose of the proceedings before the Court.

On 21 March 2002 the Court decided to communicate the application to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in 1975 and 1970 respectively.

On 19 and 20 August 1995 respectively the applicants were taken into custody by police officers from the Anti-Terrorism Department of the Aydın Security Directorate on suspicion of their membership to an illegal organisation, namely the DHKP/C.

In their police statements the applicants accepted the accusations against them.

On 22 August 1995 the applicants were brought before the investigating judge at the Aydın Magistrate’s Court, where they repeated the statements they had made to the police. The investigating judge ordered that they be placed in detention on remand.

In an indictment dated 6 September 1995, the public prosecutor at the Izmir State Security Court initiated criminal proceedings against the applicants. He charged the first applicant with being member of an armed gang under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code and the second applicant with aiding and abetting an armed gang under Article 169 of the Criminal Code.

During the hearings before the Izmir State Security Court, the applicants denied the statements they had made to the police and the investigating judge.

On 25 March 1997 the Izmir State Security Court, composed of two civilian judges and a military judge, convicted the applicants as charged and sentenced the first applicant to three years and nine months’ imprisonment and the second applicant to twelve years and six months’ imprisonment. Neither the applicants nor their lawyers were present in this last hearing.

The applicants appealed. On 12 November 1997 the Court of Cassation dismissed the applicants’ appeal, upholding the Izmir State Security Court’s reasoning and assessment of evidence. The decision, which was pronounced in the absence of the applicants and their lawyers, was deposited with the Registry of the Izmir State Security Court on 21 November 1997.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in a specific application number against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the former Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the human rights Convention”) by Turkish nationals, the first applicant and the second applicant, in late spring of a particular year.

The applicants, who had been granted legal aid, were represented by a legal representative practicing in Izmir. The respondent State's representative did not designate an Agent for the purpose of the proceedings before the European Court of Human Rights.

On a subsequent date, some years later, the European Court of Human Rights decided to communicate the application to the State's representative. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the human rights Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in the mid-1970s and the early 1970s respectively.

On two consecutive days in late summer of that year the applicants were taken into custody by police officers from a specialized anti-terrorism unit within the Aydın Security Directorate on suspicion of their membership to a designated illegal organization.

In their police statements the applicants accepted the accusations against them.

A few days later the applicants were brought before the presiding judicial official at a local judicial authority in Aydın, where they repeated the statements they had made to the police. The presiding judicial official ordered that they be placed in detention on remand.

In an indictment dated a few weeks later, a state prosecutor at the specialized security court in Izmir initiated criminal proceedings against the applicants. He charged the first applicant with being member of an armed gang under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code and the second applicant with aiding and abetting an armed gang under Article 169 of the Criminal Code.

During the hearings before the specialized security court in Izmir, the applicants denied the statements they had made to the police and the presiding judicial official.

On a date approximately a year and a half later, the specialized security court in Izmir, composed of two non-military members of the bench and a military member of the bench, convicted the applicants as charged and sentenced the first applicant to a significant period of imprisonment and the second applicant to an extended period of imprisonment. Neither the applicants nor their legal representatives were present in this last hearing.

The applicants appealed. Some months later the highest appellate court dismissed the applicants’ appeal, upholding the specialized security court in Izmir’s reasoning and assessment of evidence. The decision, which was pronounced in the absence of the applicants and their legal representatives, was deposited with the registry of the specialized security court in Izmir a week or so after that.

original:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 5136/11) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Kamil Prus (“the applicant”), on 6 December 2010.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by Mr S. Kotuła, a lawyer practising in Lublin. The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Ms J. Chrzanowska, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged, in particular, a breach of Article 3 of the Convention on account of the imposition on him of the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime.

On 7 July 2014 the application was communicated to the Government.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1987 and is detained in Lublin.

A. Criminal proceedings against the applicant

On 1 December 2005 the applicant was arrested and remanded in custody. In 2006 he was convicted of three counts of battery and robbery and received prison sentences ranging from two to four years. On 28 November 2006 the Lublin Regional Court imposed a cumulative sentence for four criminal convictions.

B. Imposition of the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime

On 5 November 2010 the Opole Lubelskie Prison Penitentiary Commission imposed on the applicant the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime. The commission held that the applicant had been the leader of or an active participant in a planned collective remonstrance in Opole Lubelskie Prison (Article 88a § 2 (2a) of the Code of Execution of Criminal Sentences), as the detainees refused to accept food provided by the prison authorities. The authorities learned that the detainees were planning another collective protest for 11 November 2010. The commission also considered that the applicant was particularly depraved.

On 18 January 2011 the Opole Lubelskie Prison Penitentiary Commission rejected an appeal lodged by the applicant and dismissed his request for leave to examine the appeal outside the statutory time-limit.

Subsequently, the applicant was transferred to the Lublin Remand Centre.

On 3 February 2011 the Lublin Remand Centre Penitentiary Commission reviewed and upheld the decision to apply the regime to the applicant, considering that he posed a serious threat to the security of the detention centre. The applicant appealed.

On 4 April 2011 the Lublin Regional Court dismissed the appeal. The court referred to the reasons given in the decision of 5 November 2010, holding that the decision had been lawful and justified.

On 3 March 2011 the applicant lodged a complaint with the Lublin Regional Inspectorate of Prisons. He complained that he was having difficulties in accessing educational activities in the Lublin Remand Centre. The authorities dismissed the complaint, holding that educational activities were not organised for dangerous prisoners.

On an unspecified date in 2011 the applicant notified the police of his allegedly unlawful classification as a “dangerous detainee” on 5 November 2010. He claimed that the Opole Lubelskie Prison authorities had exceeded their powers in imposing the regime on him. On 29 April 2011 the Opole Lubelskie District Prosecutor refused to open an investigation in the case, holding that no offence had been committed.

On 28 April 2011 the Lublin Remand Centre Penitentiary Commission revoked its decision to classify the applicant as a “dangerous detainee”. It considered that the applicant’s behaviour had improved and that he no longer posed a threat to the security of the remand centre.
anonymized_llm:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against a Member State lodged with the international human rights tribunal under a provision of the relevant human rights treaty concerning individual applications (‘‘the treaty’’) by a national of that Member State (“the applicant”), in early December of the reference year.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by a legal representative operating in a regional center. The authorities of the Member State (“the Government”) were represented by a representative from a department of the state administration.

The applicant alleged, in particular, a breach of a fundamental provision of the treaty concerning prohibited treatment on account of the imposition on him of a specialized security regime for certain inmates.

In mid-summer, several years after the reference year, the application was communicated to the Government.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in their late twenties at the time of the application and is detained in a regional urban center.

A. Criminal proceedings against the applicant

On a date in an earlier period, the applicant was arrested and remanded in custody. The subsequent year, the applicant was convicted of several charges of battery and robbery and received prison sentences ranging for a multi-year period. In late November of that conviction year, a regional court imposed a cumulative sentence for multiple criminal convictions.

B. Imposition of a specialized security regime for certain inmates

In early November of the reference year, a penitentiary commission at a specific detention facility imposed on the applicant a specialized security regime for certain inmates. The commission held that the applicant had been the leader of or an active participant in a planned collective remonstrance in that detention facility (a specific domestic legal provision related to penal enforcement), as the detainees refused to accept food provided by the prison authorities. The authorities learned that the detainees were planning another collective protest for mid-November of the reference year. The commission also considered that the applicant was particularly depraved.

Early in the year following the reference year, the penitentiary commission at the aforementioned facility rejected an appeal lodged by the applicant and dismissed his request for leave to examine the appeal outside the statutory time-limit.

Subsequently, the applicant was transferred to a different regional detention center.

Early February of the year following the reference year, the penitentiary commission at this new center reviewed and upheld the decision to apply the regime to the applicant, considering that he posed a serious threat to the security of the detention center. The applicant appealed.

Early April of the year following the reference year, the regional court dismissed the appeal. The court referred to the reasons given in the initial decision date in early November of the reference year, holding that the decision had been lawful and justified.

Early March of the year following the reference year, the applicant lodged a complaint with a regional oversight body for prisons. He complained that he was having difficulties in accessing educational activities in the regional detention center. The authorities dismissed the complaint, holding that educational activities were not organised for dangerous prisoners.

On an unspecified date in the year following the reference year, the applicant notified the police of his allegedly unlawful classification as a “dangerous detainee” on the early November decision of the reference year. He claimed that the authorities of the initial detention facility had exceeded their powers in imposing the regime on him. Late April of the year following the reference year, a regional district prosecutor refused to open an investigation in the case, holding that no offence had been committed.

On a slightly earlier date in late April of the year following the reference year, the penitentiary commission at the regional detention center revoked its decision to classify the applicant as a “dangerous detainee”. It considered that the applicant’s behaviour had improved and that he no longer posed a threat to the security of the remand centre.

original:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 57836/00) against the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a United Kingdom national, Mr Spencer James Mellors (“the applicant”), on 12 January 2000.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by Mr J. Carroll, a lawyer practising in Glasgow. The United Kingdom Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr C. Whomersley of the Foreign and Commonwealth Office, London.

The applicant alleged that his criminal proceedings exceeded a reasonable time contrary to Article 6 § 1 of the Convention.

The application was allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On 19 June 2001, the Court rejected part of the application as inadmissible.

On 1 November 2001, the Court changed the composition of its sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Third Section.

By a final decision of 30 January 2003, the Court declared the application partly admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). The Chamber decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (Rule 59 § 3 in fine).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in 1964 and is currently serving a sentence of imprisonment in HM Prison Craiginches, Aberdeen.

The applicant was arrested on a charge of attempted murder arising out of a high speed car chase which occurred in the early hours of 29 October 1995. On 30 October 1995, he was arrested again, interviewed and cautioned in respect of a separate incident of rape and assault which also occurred during the early morning of 29 October 1995.

On 31 October 1995, the applicant appeared in court in respect of the various road traffic offences and the attempted murder of a police officer. On 7 November 1995, he was fully committed and remanded in custody on those charges (of which he was later acquitted).

On 30 November 1995, the applicant appeared before the Sheriff and was committed in respect of the rape charge.

On 24 June 1996, the trial began in the High Court. On 2 July 1996, the applicant was convicted, by a majority verdict, of all charges and sentenced to 9 years 9 months' imprisonment.

On 8 July 1996, the applicant lodged an intention to appeal with the High Court sitting as an appeal court. On 20 August 1996, a Note of Appeal was lodged.

On 26 September 1996, a single judge refused the appeal. The applicant appealed.

On 31 October 1996, the Note of Appeal was allowed by three judges in restricted terms relating to the direction given by the trial judge on the special defence of alibi.

On 20 December 1996, the appeal was set down to be heard. According to the Government, it did not take place as the applicant refused to leave prison. The applicant stated that contrary to statutory authority the prison governor refused to allow him to leave the prison wearing civilian clothes instead of a prison uniform for the hearing. A further hearing was fixed for 22 May 1997. On that date, the Crown requested, and obtained, an adjournment as the Advocate Depute was engaged abroad.

On 19 November 1997, the appeal was set down to be heard. It was discontinued due to lack of court time and pressure of other business. It was noted that the appeal was complex and would last half a day.

On 13 and 14 January 1998, there was another appeal hearing. As the applicant's solicitor had become aware that the prosecution had never cited a witness, W. to appear at the trial, he had lodged and argued an additional ground of appeal on this point. Following argument, he sought to lodge further additional grounds of appeal. The appeal was continued to 13 February 1998 to enable him to do so.

On 13 February 1998, the High Court held that the failure to hear the evidence of W. resulted in unfairness and that further evidence should be heard. Lord Philip was designated as the judge to hear the evidence. On 28 May 1998, he fixed a date to take the evidence of W. On 20 August 1998, the hearing was adjourned as the applicant's counsel withdrew from acting on his behalf.

On 16 October 1998, W. gave evidence. In his note of 20 November 1998, Lord Philip gave his view of the credibility and reliability of W. The appeal was relisted for hearing on 8 January 1999 but the applicant's solicitors wrote to the court requesting that another date, possibly in the following week, be fixed due to the unavailability of counsel.

On 24 June 1999, the appeal was heard before the High Court.

On 22 July 1999, the judgment of the court was given by the Lord Justice General, Lord Rodger. It dismissed the applicant's appeal.
anonymized_llm:
PROCEDURE

The matter began with an application (bearing a specific case number) against the respondent State, lodged with the international tribunal under a foundational human rights treaty (referred to herein as "the treaty") by a national of that State, the applicant in this instance, early in that year.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by a legal representative based in a city in the west. The national government (referred to herein as "the State") was represented by an agent from a government department in the capital city.

The applicant contended that the duration of his criminal proceedings exceeded a reasonable time, contrary to a specific article of the treaty.

The application was assigned to a specific division of the international tribunal (in accordance with its procedural rules). Within that division, the reviewing panel designated to consider the matter (as per a specific article of the treaty) was formed as outlined in the procedural rules.

In mid-year of the subsequent period, the international tribunal deemed part of the application inadmissible.

Several months later in the same year, the international tribunal altered the composition of its divisions (as per its procedural rules). This matter was then allocated to the newly constituted specific division.

By a final decision early in the year following that, the international tribunal declared the application partly admissible.

Both the applicant and the State submitted observations on the merits (as per procedural rules). The reviewing panel determined, after conferring with the parties, that no hearing on the merits was necessary (in line with procedural rules).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born some decades prior to these events and is presently serving a sentence of imprisonment in a correctional facility in a northern city.

The applicant was arrested on a charge of attempted murder following a high-speed pursuit that took place in the early hours of a specific autumn day. The following day, he was arrested again, interviewed, and cautioned concerning a distinct incident of rape and assault that also transpired during the initial autumn day.

A couple of days later, the applicant appeared in court regarding various road traffic offences and the attempted murder of an officer. A week later, he was fully committed and remanded in custody on those charges (of which he was later acquitted).

Later that month, the applicant appeared before a local judicial official and was committed concerning the charge of rape.

Around mid-year in the subsequent period, the trial began in a higher appellate court. A week later, the applicant was convicted, by a majority verdict, of all charges and sentenced to a substantial period of imprisonment.

A few days later, the applicant lodged an intention to appeal with the higher appellate court sitting as an appeal court. A month later, a Note of Appeal was submitted.

The following month, a reviewing judge refused the appeal. The applicant pursued further appeal.

A month after that, the Note of Appeal was allowed by a panel of judges in restricted terms relating to the direction given by the presiding judge on the special defence of alibi.

Later in that year, the appeal was scheduled for a hearing. According to the State, it did not proceed because the applicant declined to leave the facility. The applicant asserted that, contrary to statutory authority, the facility administrator refused to permit him to leave the facility wearing civilian attire instead of a facility uniform for the hearing. Another hearing was set for mid-year of the next period. On that occasion, the prosecution requested and was granted an adjournment because a prosecuting counsel was engaged overseas.

Later that year, the appeal was again scheduled for a hearing. It was discontinued due to insufficient court time and other pressing matters. It was noted that the appeal was intricate and would require several hours.

Early in the subsequent period, over two consecutive days, another appeal hearing took place. Since the applicant's legal representative had learned that the prosecution had never called a key witness to testify at the trial, he had submitted and argued an additional ground of appeal on this matter. After argument, he sought to submit further additional grounds of appeal. The appeal was adjourned until a month later to allow him to do so.

On the same date, the higher appellate court ruled that the failure to hear the evidence of the key witness led to unfairness and that additional evidence should be considered. The designated judge was appointed to hear the evidence. Several months later, he set a date to take the evidence of the key witness. A few months after that, the hearing was adjourned because the applicant's counsel ceased to act on his behalf.

Later that year, the key witness provided evidence. In his note the following month, the designated judge presented his assessment of the credibility and reliability of the key witness. The appeal was rescheduled for a hearing early in the next period, but the applicant's legal representatives wrote to the court requesting that an alternative date, potentially in the subsequent week, be arranged due to the unavailability of counsel.

Mid-year in that period, the appeal was heard before the higher appellate court.

A month later, the judgment of the court was delivered by a high-ranking judicial official. It rejected the applicant's appeal.
